content:
It has almost nothing to do with writing more code.

3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most developers think senior engineers write better code.
That’s rarely the biggest difference.
The engineers who consistently build reliable systems don’t spend most of their time typing.
They spend an unusual amount of time trying to destroy their own ideas before anyone else can.
That single habit changes everything.
Years ago, I believed progress meant producing code quickly.
Finish the feature.
Push the commit.
Open the pull request.
Repeat.
The faster I shipped, the better developer I thought I was.
Then I started reviewing code written by engineers who had been building software for a decade or more.
Something felt strange.
Their pull requests weren’t dramatically larger.
Their syntax wasn’t magically cleaner.
Sometimes their code looked surprisingly ordinary.
The difference appeared before they wrote the first line.
They asked questions I wasn’t asking.
What happens if this request arrives twice?
What if this API becomes unavailable?
What assumptions am I making without realizing it?
Will another developer understand this six months from now?
How would I delete this feature if the business changes its mind?
They weren’t writing code.
They were interrogating it before it existed.
That habit made their software far more resilient than mine.
Most Bugs Begin Long Before the Code Exists
Developers often blame bugs on implementation.
Missing null checks.
Incorrect conditions.
Race conditions.
Forgotten edge cases.
Those are symptoms.
The real bug usually started much earlier.
It started with an assumption that nobody challenged.
“We’ll only have one user.”
“This endpoint won’t fail.”
“The API response will always include this field.”
“This component will only be used here.”
Software rarely breaks because computers misunderstand instructions.
It breaks because humans misunderstand reality.
Senior engineers know this.
That’s why they attack assumptions before they attack problems.
The Five-Minute Exercise That Changed How I Build Software
Before starting any significant feature, I now write a single question at the top of my notes.
How could this fail in production?
Then I force myself to list answers.
Not technical answers.
Real answers.
A user refreshes halfway through checkout.
Two people edit the same record.
The network disconnects.
The backend returns stale data.
Someone pastes ten thousand rows into a form.
A teammate reuses this component somewhere I never expected.
Suddenly, implementation becomes easier.
Because the hard thinking already happened.
Coding becomes transcription.
React Made Me Learn This the Hard Way
Early in my React career, I treated components like isolated pieces of UI.
If they rendered correctly, I considered the job finished.
Months later, those same components became nightmares.
State leaked between pages.
Effects triggered unexpectedly.
Props grew into twenty-line interfaces.
Simple forms became impossible to reason about.
The issue wasn’t React.
It was my thinking.
I designed components for today’s screen instead of tomorrow’s system.
Now I ask different questions.
If this component disappears tomorrow, what breaks?
Can another developer predict its behavior without reading every line?
Is this state actually local?
Could I explain this component on a whiteboard in two minutes?
Whenever the answer is no, the implementation usually needs another pass.
Productivity Isn’t About Moving Faster
Many developers measure productivity by commits.
Or hours.
Or tickets completed.
Experienced engineers measure something else.
How often do they have to revisit yesterday’s work?
Every bug fix.
Every emergency rollback.
Every confusing refactor.
Every “quick patch.”
Those are hidden productivity taxes.
Thinking deeply once is almost always cheaper than debugging repeatedly.
The keyboard rewards speed.
Production rewards clarity.
Those are very different games.
The Habit Worth Building
You don’t need another JavaScript framework.
Or another AI coding tool.
Or another productivity system.
You need a habit that forces you to challenge your own confidence.
Before every meaningful feature, pause for five minutes and ask:
“What assumptions am I making that reality might reject?”
That question won’t make you type faster.
It will make you rewrite less.
And over the course of a career, that’s what separates developers who merely ship code from engineers who build software that survives.
